+{"id":"bd-1eqo","title":"Substantive core parity roadmap for /dp/asupersync","description":"We have working kernel slices in C, but we are still far from replicating what a Rust user would reasonably call the “core functionality” of `/dp/asupersync`. This epic is the replacement for having to read the original plan markdown: it captures the intended scope, why the work matters, and how the major subsystem tracks relate.

“Core parity” here means substantially covering the Rust surfaces that make asupersync feel like asupersync: explicit `Cx` capability flow, `Scope`/structured concurrency API, runtime state/configuration, deterministic scheduler behavior, timer/deadline-driven control flow, channel family semantics, sync primitives, orchestration combinators, lab runtime/replay/oracles, and enough bytes/io/net/app scaffolding that those APIs are usable rather than isolated kernel internals.","design":"Roadmap structure:
1. Freeze a source-backed scope definition so future work is measured against `/dp/asupersync`, not memory.
2. Build outward from semantic core to public API: `Cx`/`Scope` -> runtime state/scheduler -> time/cancel -> channels/sync -> combinators -> lab/runtime evidence -> bytes/io/net/app integration.
3. Keep all parity claims evidence-backed: every subsystem track must end in unit, invariant, and where relevant conformance/lab evidence.
4. Treat higher-level systems surfaces (full HTTP/gRPC/database/distributed stacks) as later follow-on work, but keep this backlog explicit enough that we do not need to consult `PLAN_TO_PORT_ASUPERSYNC_TO_ANSI_C.md` for the near-to-mid-term core program.","acceptance_criteria":"A. The backlog contains explicit epics and tasks for every major subsystem required to reach substantive Rust-core parity.
B. Each issue is self-contained: background, intended approach, acceptance criteria, and rationale are stored in bead fields.
C. Dependency edges are strong enough that `br ready` surfaces the intended implementation order.
D. A future contributor can reconstruct the port strategy from the bead graph alone without reopening the original markdown planning documents.","notes":"Source surfaces used to define this epic: `/dp/asupersync/README.md`, `/dp/asupersync/src/lib.rs`, `/dp/asupersync/Cargo.toml`, and the local canonical docs `docs/EXISTING_ASUPERSYNC_STRUCTURE.md`, `docs/FEATURE_PARITY.md`, and `PLAN_TO_PORT_ASUPERSYNC_TO_ANSI_C.md`.

Out of immediate scope but intentionally remembered: full HTTP/2-3, gRPC, TLS, database clients, messaging, distributed/remote/web surfaces, and the large actor/supervision tree. Those are not forgotten; they are simply downstream of the core parity tracks created here.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:40.382303984Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:40.441264230Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["core-parity","roadmap","rust-reference"]}
+{"id":"bd-1eqo.1","title":"Freeze Rust-core scope and gap inventory against /dp/asupersync","description":"Create a concrete, source-backed inventory of what counts as “core functionality” in `/dp/asupersync` today, using the Rust module graph and README guarantees rather than the earlier kernel-only wave assumptions. This task exists to stop the project from declaring victory too early.","design":"Inventory must group the Rust source by semantic track: capability context, runtime/scheduler/state, time/deadline, channels/sync, combinators, lab/replay/oracles, bytes/codec/io/net/app, and actor/supervision follow-on.

Each group should identify:
- the Rust source modules involved,
- the current C implementation status,
- the missing public API or runtime behavior,
- whether parity evidence can be unit-only, invariant-only, or requires lab/conformance artifacts.","acceptance_criteria":"A. We have a bead-backed scope definition for “core parity” that references actual `/dp/asupersync` modules.
B. Every downstream epic in this roadmap can point back to an explicit inventory slice.
C. Missing areas are described in terms of user-visible semantics/API, not only internal implementation files.","notes":"Reasoning: the current local parity matrix says the kernel units are implemented, but `/dp/asupersync/src/lib.rs` exports a much broader “core” story. This task is the guardrail against mistaking kernel completion for product-level parity.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:40.515952264Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:40.676067789Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["core-parity","inventory","planning"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:40.515952264Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"bd-1eqo.10","title":"Actor, gen_server, and supervision follow-on parity","description":"The Rust crate exports actor/gen_server/supervision surfaces that are far beyond the current C port. They are not immediate kernel prerequisites, but they are important enough to record now so they do not vanish from planning once the lower-level work starts.","design":"This epic is intentionally downstream. It exists to make the future direction explicit: once the structured concurrency, channel, combinator, and lab layers exist, we can layer actor-style APIs and supervision semantics on top in a controlled, dependency-backed way.","acceptance_criteria":"A. Actor/supervision work is visible in the backlog instead of being an undocumented “later maybe”.
B. Dependencies point back to the lower-level tracks required first.
C. Future contributors can start this track without reopening old planning documents.","notes":"Primary Rust references: `/dp/asupersync/src/actor.rs`, `gen_server.rs`, `supervision/*`, plus related messaging/session surfaces.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:49.992319567Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:54.959044613Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["actor","follow-on","supervision"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.10","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:49.992319567Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.10","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:54.958184338Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

B. Mailbox and request/reply semantics are deterministic and testable.
C. Cancellation and quiescence interact correctly with actor shutdown.","notes":"This should not start until the lower-level context/runtime/channel/combinator tracks are solid.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:50.565275409Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-12T16:55:03.420440536Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["actor","follow-on","gen-server"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.10.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo.10","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:50.565275409Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.10.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo.3.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:55:00.896483829Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.10.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo.4.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:55:03.419536197Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.10.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo.6.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:55:01.670286809Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.10.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo.7.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:55:02.607629699Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"bd-1eqo.10.2","title":"Implement supervision trees, restart strategies, and failure propagation semantics","description":"Implement supervision trees and restart semantics so actor-style workloads can recover according to explicit strategies rather than ad-hoc host logic.","design":"Scope includes:
- supervisor tree model,
- restart strategies and escalation,
- interaction with lab replay/evidence,
- tests for child failure, restart ordering, and shutdown propagation.","acceptance_criteria":"A. Supervision policies are explicit and test-backed.
B. Failure/restart behavior composes with cancellation and quiescence rules.
C. Deterministic evidence exists for supervisor decisions in replay/lab scenarios.","notes":"This is the final “core-plus” layer in this roadmap and intentionally depends on nearly every lower-level track.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:51.168021913Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-12T16:55:06.399562895Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["follow-on","restart","supervision"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.10.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo.10","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:51.168021913Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.10.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo.10.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:55:04.373722770Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.10.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo.7.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:55:05.380551321Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.10.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo.8.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:55:06.398594015Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"bd-1eqo.2","title":"Convert the core inventory into a fixture/provenance coverage plan","description":"Turn the source inventory into a fixture/provenance plan so that future parity claims for new core surfaces are evidence-backed from the start, not retrofitted after code lands.","design":"For each new core track, define what proof artifacts are required: unit tests, invariant suites, lab scenarios, replay evidence, JSON/BIN equivalence, or Rust-vs-C semantic fixtures. Extend the existing provenance approach used by the kernel docs to the broader core backlog.","acceptance_criteria":"A. Each implementation epic has an explicit verification model before coding begins.
B. New core surfaces have a named provenance path from Rust source -> C behavior -> acceptance evidence.
C. The backlog itself records where fixture debt exists so it cannot be silently skipped.","notes":"This is the bridge between planning and implementation. Without it, broad API work will accumulate shallow tests and no durable parity proof.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:40.762430293Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:55.447459498Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fixtures","parity-evidence","planning"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:40.762430293Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:55.446591849Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"bd-1eqo.3","title":"Capability context and structured concurrency public API parity","description":"Rust asupersync is not just a scheduler; it is a capability-secure async model built around explicit `Cx` flow and scoped ownership. Without this surface, the ANSI C port cannot honestly claim to replicate core usage patterns.","design":"This epic covers the public API layer that carries cancellation, budgets, clocks, and effect authority through every async operation. It should produce a C-facing equivalent that makes effect authority explicit rather than ambient, and that composes with the existing lifecycle kernel.","acceptance_criteria":"A. The C port exposes an explicit capability/context model rather than only global helpers.
B. Structured concurrency entrypoints are expressed in terms of owned scopes/regions and joinable task handles.
C. Downstream channel, sync, combinator, and time APIs can consistently accept and use this context.","notes":"Primary Rust references: `/dp/asupersync/src/cx/cx.rs`, `cx/scope.rs`, `cx/registry.rs`, `cx/cap.rs`, plus README examples showing `Cx`-threaded usage.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:41.013705656Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:51.795096365Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["api","core-parity","cx"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:41.013705656Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:51.794235138Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"bd-1eqo.3.1","title":"Implement Cx-style capability context and explicit authority flow","description":"Implement the public `Cx` equivalent and its minimal capability registry so that time reads, cancellation checkpoints, channel reserves, sync acquires, and other effects stop depending on ambient global state.","design":"Scope includes:
- public context type and lifecycle,
- budget/cancel/clock accessors,
- authority/capability plumbing sufficient for runtime, channel, sync, and combinator APIs,
- explicit no-ambient-authority rules in headers/docs/tests.","acceptance_criteria":"A. Core async-facing APIs can be expressed in terms of a passed context/capability handle.
B. Context carries deterministic-time and cancellation information needed by downstream tracks.
C. Unit tests prove APIs reject missing/invalid authority rather than silently reading globals.","notes":"This is the semantic doorway into the rest of the runtime. If it is wrong, later parity work will be forced into wrappers and compatibility shims we explicitly do not want.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:41.269276217Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-12T16:54:39.849483964Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["capabilities","core-parity","cx"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.3.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:55.929907733Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.3.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo.3","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:41.269276217Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"bd-1eqo.3.2","title":"Implement Scope, task handles, and join semantics on top of region ownership","description":"Implement the scoped public concurrency API: nested scope/region entrypoints, owned child task spawning, join/task-handle semantics, and outcome/join-error mapping that mirrors the Rust user model rather than the current kernel-only internals.","design":"Scope includes:
- `Scope`/region nesting API,
- task handle lifecycle and join semantics,
- region-close-to-quiescence behavior surfaced as user-facing contract,
- outcome mapping for completion, cancellation, and panic/error analogues.","acceptance_criteria":"A. Users can express structured child-task ownership without touching internal scheduler tables.
B. Join/task-handle semantics are specified and tested.
C. Scope exit guarantees quiescence and no-orphan-task behavior in public-facing tests/examples.","notes":"Primary Rust references: README “quick example”, `/dp/asupersync/src/cx/scope.rs`, and task/region handle behavior implied by the exported API.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:41.529159071Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-12T16:54:40.173377697Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["core-parity","scope","task-handle"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.3.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo.3","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:41.529159071Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.3.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo.3.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:56.448960183Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"bd-1eqo.4","title":"Runtime object, scheduler, and state parity","description":"The current C runtime proves kernel semantics in isolation, but Rust asupersync’s core runtime also includes a richer runtime object, scheduler architecture, priority behavior, and state plumbing. This epic closes that gap.","design":"The runtime track must evolve the current walking-skeleton kernel into something that can faithfully host the public API: configuration/state objects, deterministic multi-lane scheduling, inject paths, fairness/preemption behavior, and optional parallel/stealing variants without semantic drift.","acceptance_criteria":"A. Runtime lifecycle/config/state objects exist and are testable as first-class surfaces.
B. Scheduler behavior is described in terms close to the Rust runtime rather than a minimal proof-of-concept loop.
C. Fairness, priority, injector, and optional stealing/parallel behavior are backed by tests or lab evidence.","notes":"Primary Rust references: `/dp/asupersync/src/runtime/config.rs`, `runtime/state.rs`, `runtime/scheduler/mod.rs`, `runtime/scheduler/three_lane.rs`, `runtime/scheduler/priority.rs`, `runtime/scheduler/global_injector.rs`, `runtime/scheduler/stealing.rs`.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:41.825741213Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:52.225252520Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["core-parity","runtime","scheduler"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:41.825741213Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:52.224475852Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"bd-1eqo.4.1","title":"Implement runtime config, runtime object lifecycle, and state query surfaces","description":"Implement runtime configuration/state surfaces that match the Rust core more closely: builder/defaulting behavior, runtime object lifecycle, and state/query plumbing needed by higher-level APIs.","design":"Scope includes:
- runtime config defaults and validation,
- runtime creation/destroy/bootstrap API,
- state tables and query surfaces needed by Cx/scope/time/channel/combinator tracks,
- profile/resource defaults expressed through runtime object rather than only compile-time globals.","acceptance_criteria":"A. Public/runtime tests can create a runtime object with explicit config.
B. State/query APIs needed by downstream tracks exist and are covered.
C. Runtime bootstrap no longer depends on hidden global initialization order.","notes":"This task is the structural prerequisite for meaningful public API parity and for moving beyond “kernel library plus globals”.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:42.118256776Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:57.490844714Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["core-parity","runtime-config","state"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.4.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:56.973945512Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.4.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo.3.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:57.489942010Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.4.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo.4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:42.118256776Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"bd-1eqo.4.2","title":"Implement three-lane scheduler, priority/injector behavior, and parity-safe parallel execution","description":"Replace the minimal scheduler shape with a deterministic architecture closer to Rust core behavior: lane selection, priority scheduling, global injection, and optional work stealing/parallel execution paths that preserve semantic determinism.","design":"Scope includes:
- deterministic lane/runnable queue model,
- priority and fairness rules,
- injector path and starvation/preemption evidence,
- optional multi-worker semantics behind profiles without changing observable behavior for fixed scenarios.","acceptance_criteria":"A. Scheduler tests cover lane ordering, priority effects, fairness, and inject behavior.
B. Single-worker deterministic replay remains stable while richer scheduling behavior is added.
C. Any parallel/stealing path has parity tests proving it does not fork semantics.","notes":"This is where the runtime stops being merely correct and starts resembling the real operating shape of `/dp/asupersync`.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:42.416492019Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:58.611132035Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["core-parity","fairness","scheduler"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.4.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo.3.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:58.610228048Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.4.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo.4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:42.416492019Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.4.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo.4.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:58.066664537Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"bd-1eqo.5","title":"Time, deadlines, and cancellation-driver parity","description":"Rust asupersync exposes time as a semantic control plane: deadlines, sleeps, timeouts, intervals, and bounded drain/cancel behavior all interact. The ANSI C port currently has timer primitives but not the whole user-facing time contract.","design":"This epic extends the current timer wheel into a full time/deadline/cancellation story: runtime timer driver, public time-based APIs, and explicit progress evidence for draining/quiescence under cancellation.","acceptance_criteria":"A. Time-based user APIs exist and are wired through the runtime/capability model.
B. Deadline-driven cancellation and bounded cleanup are observable in tests.
C. Quiescence/drain evidence surfaces exist where the Rust design promises them.","notes":"Primary Rust references: `/dp/asupersync/src/time/wheel.rs`, `time/driver.rs`, `time/deadline.rs`, and README discussion of bounded cleanup and drain progress certificates.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:42.754630594Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:52.674095558Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["cancel","core-parity","time"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.5","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:42.754630594Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1eqo.5","depends_on_id":"bd-1eqo.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T16:53:52.673227147Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"bd-1eqo.5.1","title":"Implement deadline abstractions and public sleep/timeout/interval APIs","description":"Implement the runtime timer-driver and deadline abstraction that higher-level APIs expect, then surface `sleep`, `timeout`, and `interval` primitives through the public capability/runtime model.","design":"Scope includes:
- deadline objects and budget integration,
- timer-driver/runtime integration beyond raw wheel operations,
- public sleep/timeout/interval APIs with deterministic behavior in normal and lab runtimes.","acceptance_criteria":"A. Public time APIs exist and are tested in deterministic scenarios.

 {"id":"bd-1md","title":"EPIC: Conformance, Differential Fuzzing, and Test Completeness","description":"Background:
Port correctness is proven, not asserted. We need fixture parity, stochastic drift detection, and detailed diagnostics for fast triage.

Goal:
Build full test stack: unit, invariant, stress, e2e, conformance, and differential fuzzing with minimization.

Key requirement:
Tests and scripts must produce rich logs/artifacts so failures are actionable and reproducible.

Why this serves users:
- high confidence in semantic faithfulness
- lower MTTR for regressions
- safer optimization velocity.

+{"id":"bd-aw69","title":"EPIC: Embedded Target Matrix Gate (GATE-EMBEDDED)","description":"## Goal
Implement GATE-EMBEDDED: cross-compilation for ARM and RISC-V targets with QEMU-based smoke
tests, validating that the C port builds and runs correctly on constrained embedded platforms.

## Why This Matters
The C port's raison d'être is portability to embedded targets that Rust's async runtime cannot
reach (bare-metal MCUs, router-class devices, automotive ECUs). Without actually building and
testing on these targets, the "portable ANSI C" claim is aspirational rather than proven.

GATE-EMBEDDED is one of the 9 non-negotiable quality gates from AGENTS.md:
"Embedded target matrix gate (cross-build + QEMU + device smoke)."

The three resource classes (R1=tight, R2=balanced, R3=roomy) defined in asx_config.h are
specifically designed for embedded deployment. The FREESTANDING and EMBEDDED_ROUTER profiles
compile with no libc dependency. This gate proves those profiles work end-to-end.

## What "Done" Looks Like
- Cross-compilation succeeds for: arm-none-eabi (Cortex-M), aarch64-linux-gnu, riscv32-unknown-elf
- QEMU user-mode runs the C test suite for aarch64 and riscv32 targets
- Bare-metal builds (arm-none-eabi) link successfully with FREESTANDING profile
- Resource class R1 builds fit within 64KB RAM budget
- make embedded-matrix passes in CI

## Architectural Context
The Makefile already has profile-parity targets that build with ASX_PROFILE_FREESTANDING and
ASX_PROFILE_EMBEDDED_ROUTER. The freestanding hooks (src/platform/freestanding/hooks.c) provide
stub implementations for platforms without libc. What's missing is the actual cross-compiler
toolchains, QEMU setup, and the CI integration to exercise them.

## Risk
- Cross-compiler toolchain installation varies by host OS
- QEMU user-mode may not perfectly emulate all target quirks
- Bare-metal linking requires a linker script and startup code
- R1 memory budget (64KB) may require tuning ASX_MAX_* constants","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","estimated_minutes":720,"created_at":"2026-03-12T17:06:25.215072286Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-12T17:06:25.215072286Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["embedded","phase3","portability"]}

+{"id":"bd-reg6","title":"EPIC: Rust Reference Fixture Capture Pipeline","description":"## Goal

+{"id":"bd-reg6.5","title":"Run C conformance against Rust reference fixtures (first Rust-vs-C parity run)","description":"## Goal
Execute the definitive Rust-vs-C parity check: run make conformance with --fixtures-root
pointing at the newly-captured Rust reference fixtures.

## Why This Is The Most Important Task In The Epic
This is the moment of truth. Every prior task was setup for this: does the C port produce
semantically identical outputs to the Rust reference for every scenario? A pass here
means provably equivalent behavior. A failure means we have real divergence to investigate.

## Detailed Steps
1. Run: tools/ci/run_conformance.sh --mode conformance --fixtures-root fixtures/rust_reference
2. Capture the conformance report (JSONL + summary JSON + semantic delta artifact)
3. If semantic_delta_count == 0: full parity confirmed
4. If semantic_delta_count > 0: for each delta, classify per SEMANTIC_DELTA_BUDGET.md taxonomy:
   - none: no real delta (harness artifact)
   - intentional_upstream: Rust behavior we intentionally diverge from (requires written ruling)
   - c_regression: bug in C port (must fix)
   - spec_defect: ambiguity in the spec (requires written ruling)
   - harness_defect: bug in the test harness (must fix)

+{"id":"bd-rkql","title":"EPIC: Differential Fuzzing Parity Gate (GATE-FUZZ)","description":"## Goal
Implement GATE-FUZZ: differential fuzzing that runs the same randomized inputs through both the
Rust reference implementation and the C port, comparing outputs for semantic equivalence.

## Why This Matters
Unit tests and conformance fixtures cover known scenarios. Differential fuzzing discovers UNKNOWN
divergences — edge cases where the C port's behavior differs from Rust under unusual input
combinations. This is the gold standard for porting correctness: if a fuzzer can't find a
difference after millions of iterations, confidence in behavioral equivalence is very high.

GATE-FUZZ is one of the 9 non-negotiable quality gates defined in AGENTS.md (lines 249-259):
"Differential fuzzing gate (Rust vs C + minimized counterexamples)."

## What "Done" Looks Like
- Fuzz harnesses exist for all major subsystems (lifecycle, channel, timer, scheduler, codec)
- A differential driver feeds identical inputs to Rust and C, compares outputs
- Counterexample minimization pipeline reduces failing inputs to minimal reproducers
- make fuzz-parity runs a smoke campaign (bounded iterations) that passes
- CI integration via the fuzz-parity blocking gate

## Architectural Context
The deterministic mode (ASX_DETERMINISTIC=1) ensures that for identical inputs and seeds,
both implementations should produce byte-identical trace digests. The trace digest (FNV-1a
hash chain) is the primary comparison point. The existing asx_trace_digest() and
asx_hindsight_digest() functions provide the comparison surface.

## Dependencies
Requires the Rust reference fixture capture pipeline (Epic 1) to be functional, since the
differential driver needs to invoke both the Rust binary and the C binary.

## Risk
- Requires building and running Rust and C in the same pipeline
- Fuzz campaigns are compute-intensive; CI time budget must be bounded
- Any found divergence requires root-cause analysis and classification per SEMANTIC_DELTA_BUDGET.md","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","estimated_minutes":960,"created_at":"2026-03-12T17:06:08.461751224Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-12T17:06:08.461751224Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["conformance","fuzzing","phase3"]}
+{"id":"bd-rkql.1","title":"Design fuzz harness architecture and input grammar","description":"## Goal
Design the fuzzing infrastructure: how inputs are generated, how they map to API call sequences,
and how outputs are compared between Rust and C.

## Context
Differential fuzzing for a stateful runtime is harder than fuzzing a pure function. The fuzzer
must generate valid sequences of API calls (open region, spawn task, run scheduler, drain, etc.)
that exercise the state machine without hitting trivially-invalid paths.

## Design Decisions To Make
1. **Input grammar:** Structure-aware fuzzing (grammar-based) vs raw byte mutation
   - Recommended: Grammar-based using a scenario DSL that maps bytes to API call sequences
   - The existing scenario_dsl_version field in fixtures suggests a DSL already exists
2. **Comparison surface:** What outputs to compare?
   - Primary: asx_trace_digest() — FNV-1a hash chain over all trace events
   - Secondary: final snapshot digest, obligation states, task outcomes
   - Tertiary: asx_hindsight_digest() for nondeterminism boundary events
3. **Fuzzer engine:** libFuzzer (LLVM), AFL++, or custom
   - Recommended: libFuzzer for C side (integrated with sanitizers), custom driver for Rust
4. **Seed corpus:** Use existing conformance fixtures as starting points
5. **Iteration budget:** CI smoke = 10K iterations; deep = 1M+ (nightly)

## Deliverable
A design document (docs/FUZZ_PARITY_DESIGN.md) covering input grammar, comparison protocol,
minimization strategy, CI integration plan, and iteration budgets.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Design document reviewed and approved
- Input grammar covers all major API surfaces
- Comparison protocol identifies the minimum output needed to detect divergence","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":120,"created_at":"2026-03-12T17:08:25.699065858Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-12T17:08:25.699065858Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["design","fuzzing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-rkql.1","depends_on_id":"bd-rkql","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-12T17:08:25.699065858Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

Create libFuzzer-compatible fuzz harnesses that exercise the C API through structured input.

## Subsystem Harnesses Needed
1. **fuzz_lifecycle.c** — Region open/close/drain, task spawn/cancel/finalize, obligation lifecycle
2. **fuzz_channel.c** — Channel create, reserve/send/abort, recv, close sender/receiver
3. **fuzz_timer.c** — Timer register/cancel/update/collect, deadline ordering
4. **fuzz_scheduler.c** — Scheduler run with varying budgets, task poll sequences
5. **fuzz_codec.c** — Encode/decode fixture round-trip, cross-codec equivalence
6. **fuzz_integrated.c** — Full scenario execution: region+tasks+obligations+scheduler+drain

## Implementation Pattern
Each harness follows this pattern:
```c
int LLVMFuzzerTestOneInput(const uint8_t *data, size_t size) {
    asx_runtime_reset();
    // Interpret data bytes as API call sequence using the input grammar
    // Execute the sequence
    // Capture trace digest
    return 0;
}
```

## Why Per-Subsystem + Integrated
Per-subsystem harnesses find subsystem-specific bugs faster (smaller state space).
The integrated harness finds cross-subsystem interaction bugs (larger but more realistic).

+{"id":"bd-rkql.3","title":"Implement Rust-side fuzz target using same input grammar","description":"## Goal
Create a Rust fuzz target that accepts the SAME input grammar as the C harnesses and produces
comparable trace digests, enabling differential comparison.

## Key Requirement: Identical Input Interpretation
The Rust target must interpret the fuzzer-generated byte sequence identically to the C harness.
The input grammar specification (from bd-rkql.1) is the shared contract. Both sides must:
- Parse the same byte sequence into the same API call sequence
- Execute with the same seed (derived from input)
- Produce a trace digest that can be compared

## Implementation
- Use cargo-fuzz (libFuzzer backend) or a custom Rust binary
- Accept raw bytes on stdin, parse via shared grammar, execute, print digest
- Output format: single line with hex trace digest for comparison

## Why The Rust Side Is Simpler
The Rust implementation is the reference — we trust its behavior. The Rust target just needs
to execute and emit a digest. The C side is the one being validated against it.

- Rust fuzz target compiles and runs against pinned baseline

+{"id":"bd-rkql.4","title":"Build differential fuzz driver comparing Rust vs C digests","description":"## Goal
Create the differential driver that orchestrates fuzzing: generates inputs, feeds them to both
Rust and C targets, and flags any divergent outputs.

## Architecture
```
[Fuzzer Engine] → random bytes
    ├── [C Harness] → C trace digest
    └── [Rust Target] → Rust trace digest
    → Compare: if C_digest != Rust_digest → DIVERGENCE FOUND → minimize
```

## Implementation Options
1. **Shell-based driver:** For each input, run both binaries, diff digests
   - Simple but slow (process spawn overhead per iteration)
2. **In-process with shared library:** Load C as .so, Rust as .so, call both in same process
   - Fast but complex linking
3. **AFL++ custom mutator + dual target:** Use AFL++'s differential mode
   - Moderate complexity, good performance

Recommended: Start with option 1 (shell-based) for correctness, optimize to option 2 if
iteration speed becomes a bottleneck.

## Counterexample Handling
When a divergence is found:
1. Save the raw input bytes to fuzz_findings/{timestamp}_{hash}.bin
2. Log both digests and the API call sequence interpretation
3. Trigger the minimization pipeline (bd-rkql.5)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Driver correctly identifies known-good inputs (same digest) and synthetic divergences
- At least 1000 iterations complete without false positives on known-good code
- Divergences are saved with full reproducibility information","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":180,"created_at":"2026-03-12T17:09:07.604910607Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-12T17:09:07.604910607Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fuzzing","implementation"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-rkql.4","depends_on_id":"bd-rkql","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-12T17:09:07.604910607Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"bd-rkql.5","title":"Implement counterexample minimization pipeline","description":"## Goal
When the differential driver finds a divergence, automatically reduce the input to the smallest
byte sequence that still triggers the divergence.

## Why Minimization Matters
Raw fuzzer inputs are typically large and contain irrelevant bytes. A 4KB input that triggers
a divergence might minimize to 12 bytes that expose a single off-by-one error. Minimal
reproducers are vastly easier to debug and classify.

## Implementation
1. Use delta debugging (ddmin algorithm): iteratively remove bytes while divergence persists
2. Or use libFuzzer's built-in -minimize_crash=1 flag
3. Output: minimal input + annotated API call sequence + both digests

## Detailed Steps
1. Accept a divergence input file
2. Binary-search reduce: remove halves, quarters, individual bytes
3. At each step, re-run both Rust and C to check if divergence persists
4. Output minimal_*.bin + minimal_*.txt (human-readable call sequence)

## Integration
- Called automatically by the differential driver when divergence is found
- Results go to fuzz_findings/minimized/
- Each minimized finding becomes a candidate for a new bead (priority 0 bug)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Minimization reduces synthetic test divergence from >100 bytes to <20 bytes
- Minimized inputs are deterministically reproducible
- Human-readable annotation of the minimal API call sequence","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":120,"created_at":"2026-03-12T17:09:21.749610920Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-12T17:09:21.749610920Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fuzzing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-rkql.5","depends_on_id":"bd-rkql","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-12T17:09:21.749610920Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

+{"id":"bd-va8n","title":"EPIC: Local Lint and Format Tooling (GATE-CHECK)","description":"## Goal
Install and configure clang-format and clang-tidy locally so that make format-check and
make lint produce real results instead of SKIP.

GATE-CHECK (format + lint + strict build) is the first CI blocking gate. Currently these
targets skip locally because the tools are not installed, meaning developers and agents
cannot catch formatting or lint violations before pushing. This creates a feedback gap
where CI rejects commits that looked clean locally.

## What "Done" Looks Like
- clang-format (version matching CI) installed and on PATH
- clang-tidy (version matching CI) installed and on PATH
- make format-check runs and either passes or reports real violations
- make lint runs and either passes or reports real violations
- .clang-format and .clang-tidy configs match CI expectations

## Risk
- Low risk. This is purely toolchain installation.
